skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Manage Git workflows including commits, branches, merges, and collaboration. Use when working with Git repositories, creating commits, managing branches, or resolving conflicts.
skill.summary: Manage Git workflows including commits, branches, merges, and collaboration. Use when working with G...
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: This skill provides documentation for git workflows. All static findings are false positives: external_commands detections are bash examples in markdown, network detections are reference URLs in documentation links, and weak_crypto detections incorrectly flag 'git' and branch naming conventions as cryptographic algorithms. No executable code with security risks present.

content.value_statement: This skill helps developers manage Git workflows including commits, branches, merges, and collaboration. It provides step-by-step instructions and best practices for version control operations.
actual_capabilities: Create and manage feature branches with proper naming conventions
Write meaningful commit messages following conventional commits standard
Resolve merge conflicts using git tools and commands
Perform interactive rebase to clean up commit history
Use advanced git commands like cherry-pick and bisect
limitations: Does not provide interactive git GUI or visual interface
Does not integrate with specific git hosting platforms APIs
Cannot automatically resolve complex merge conflicts without user input
Requires user to have git installed on their system

use_cases.title: Feature Development Workflow
use_cases.description: Create feature branches, make commits with good messages, keep branch updated with main, and push changes for code review
use_cases.target_user: Software developers working on new features

use_cases.title: Hotfix Production Issues
use_cases.description: Quickly create hotfix branches from main, make urgent fixes, and merge with proper commit messages documenting the fix

use_cases.title: Collaborative Team Development
use_cases.description: Coordinate with team members, manage shared branches, resolve conflicts, and maintain clean commit history through rebasing

prompt_templates.title: Create Feature Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the git-workflow skill to create a new feature branch from main called feature/user-authentication
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new feature development task

prompt_templates.title: Write Good Commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a good commit message for adding user login functionality. The changes include: adding login form component, implementing JWT token generation, and adding logout endpoint.

prompt_templates.title: Resolve Merge Conflict
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a merge conflict in my feature branch after pulling from main. The conflict is in the file src/auth/login.js. Help me resolve it.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Up Commit History
prompt_templates.prompt: I have 5 small commits in my feature branch with messages like 'wip', 'fix', 'typo'. Help me squash them into one meaningful commit using interactive rebase.
prompt_templates.scenario: Cleaning up commit history before merge

output_examples.input: Create a feature branch for adding dark mode
output_examples.output:
To create a feature branch for dark mode:

1. Switch to main branch: git checkout main
2. Pull latest: git pull origin main
3. Create feature branch: git checkout -b feature/dark-mode

Branch naming convention: feature/description

output_examples.input: Write a commit message for fixing login bug
output_examples.output:
Good commit message format:

fix(auth): fix login redirect bug

- Fix redirect loop when credentials are invalid
- Add proper error message display
- Update redirect logic after successful login

Closes #123

best_practices: Commit small, focused changes with descriptive messages that explain what and why
Use feature branches for all work; never commit directly to main branch
Rebase feature branches before merging to keep history clean and linear
anti_patterns: Do not commit large, unrelated changes in a single commit
Avoid force pushing to shared branches without coordination
Never skip code review by merging directly to main without pull requests

faq.question: What is the difference between merge and rebase?
faq.answer: Merge combines branch histories by creating a merge commit. Rebase replays your commits on top of another branch, creating a linear history. Use rebase for clean history on feature branches, merge for integrating completed work.

faq.question: When should I use force push?
faq.answer: Only use force push on personal feature branches that nobody else is working on. Use --force-with-lease for safety. Never force push to shared branches like main.

faq.question: How do I undo a commit?
faq.answer: Use git reset --soft HEAD~1 to undo and keep changes staged. Use git reset --hard HEAD~1 to discard changes completely. If already pushed, use git revert to create a new commit that undoes the changes.

faq.question: What are conventional commits?
faq.answer: Conventional commits format: type(scope): description. Types include feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore. Example: feat(auth): add login endpoint. This enables automated versioning and changelog generation.

faq.question: How do I resolve merge conflicts?
faq.answer: Run git status to see conflicted files. Open each file and look for conflict markers (<<<<, ====, >>>>). Keep the desired changes, remove markers, then git add the file and complete the merge with git commit.

faq.question: What is the best branch naming convention?
faq.answer: Use prefixes: feature/description for new features, bugfix/description for bug fixes, hotfix/description for urgent production fixes, refactor/description for code improvements, docs/description for documentation changes.
